_instructions: Add your own real posts here as golden reference examples. The evals use these to judge tone and style alignment. Add as many as you like per category — more examples = better calibration.

instagram.caption:
There's a reason people stop mid-sentence when a male Cardinal lands nearby.

That shock of crimson isn't just beautiful — it's a biological statement. Males grow more intensely red in winter, when females are most actively choosing mates. The redder the bird, the better the provider. It's vanity with evolutionary purpose.

What most people don't know is that Cardinals are one of the few songbirds where the female sings too. You might hear her calling from the nest, a soft metallic chip that her mate learns to recognise as his alone.

They're also fiercely loyal to a place, not just a partner. A Cardinal pair may hold the same half-acre of yard for their entire lives — raising clutch after clutch of chicks in the same hedgerow while you go about your seasons.

Next time one lands in your garden, look closely at the crest. If it's raised, something has its attention. If it's flat, it's relaxed. You're being trusted.

Have you spotted a Cardinal in your yard this week? Tell us below 👇

instagram.caption:
On warm June evenings, something ancient is happening in the tall grass.

Fireflies — also called lightning bugs — produce one of nature's most efficient light sources. Their bioluminescence converts nearly 100% of energy into light with almost no heat, a feat our best LED bulbs still can't match. Scientists call it 'cold light.' We just call it magic.

Each species flashes its own private code. Males signal from the air; females respond from the grass below. Some flash in perfect synchrony — entire forests of them pulsing together like a single heartbeat. In the Great Smoky Mountains, thousands gather each year to do exactly this, and people travel from around the world just to watch.

But here's the part that should make us pay attention: firefly populations are declining in many regions, hit hard by light pollution, habitat loss, and pesticide use. The nights are getting quieter.

If you have a garden, turn off the outdoor lights for a single evening this summer. You might be surprised who shows up.

Do you still get fireflies where you live? Let us know in the comments 👇

instagram.caption:
Long before it was a candle scent or a face cream, lavender was a medicine chest.

Romans added it to their bathwater — the name comes from the Latin lavare, 'to wash.' Medieval healers tucked it into plague masks, believing its scent could ward off disease. They were onto something: lavender's essential oil contains linalool and linalyl acetate, compounds with genuine antimicrobial and calming properties backed by modern research.

As a botanical, lavender has earned its place twice over. Herbalists use it for anxiety, insomnia, and tension headaches. Apiculturists plant whole fields of it because its nectar produces some of the most prized honey in the world. And in Provence, France, entire economies were built around its annual bloom.

What most people miss is how long a single plant lives. A well-tended lavender can thrive for decades — the same woody shrub filling your garden with scent summer after summer, getting better with age.

Do you grow lavender? Drop a photo in the comments — we'd love to see it 🌿👇
